topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee tasted like ash in my mouth. I slammed the mug onto the chipped Formica table, the sudden noise echoing in the otherwise quiet office. Another Tuesday, another soul seeking solace from their past. I watched Dr. Silas Thorne lean back in his leather chair, his expression a practiced blend of deep empathy and something sharper, something I hadn't seen since I was twelve. He was talking to a young woman about inherited trauma, his voice a low, soothing balm that grated on my nerves. I shifted in my seat, the cheap fabric of my jacket scratching my neck. I wanted to be anywhere else, anywhere but this sterile room filled with manufactured calm. When the session finally ended, the woman thanked him profusely, and Silas offered a small, almost imperceptible nod. I just stared at my hands, feeling the familiar tightness coil in my chest.

[story 2]

My shoulders were hunched so tightly I thought my spine might crack. Every interaction felt like wading through thick mud. I kept my gaze fixed on the scuff marks on the floor, counting the imperfections. Then, the door opened, and there he was. Silas. He looked exactly the same—that infuriatingly composed set to his jaw, the way he held himself like he owned the very air in the room. He was reviewing a file, his movements economical and precise. When his eyes flicked up and met mine, there was a flicker—a ghost of that old, challenging smirk that used to make my stomach clench with pure irritation. I didn't look away immediately; I just held his gaze, letting the tension build until it was almost unbearable. "Having a good day, Silas?" I managed, my voice sounding rougher than I intended. He merely raised an eyebrow, a silent acknowledgment that somehow managed to feel both deeply familiar and profoundly unwelcome.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights hummed with an irritating, incessant drone that seemed to vibrate directly against my skull. I picked at a loose thread on my sleeve, my jaw clenched so hard my molars ached. I was waiting for a consultation, and the sheer forced pleasantness of the atmosphere was actively making me want to bolt. Then, Silas walked in. He moved with an effortless grace that always seemed out of place in this environment of forced vulnerability. He paused near the reception desk, and I caught a glimpse of him—older, undeniably more polished, yet the core of that sharp, uncompromising energy remained. A low, involuntary scoff escaped my lips. He caught the look, and for a split second, the practiced mask slipped, revealing something raw, something undeniably reminiscent of the sharp-tongued kid who used to dominate the playground. I immediately dropped my eyes, pulling my arms tighter across my chest, trying to shrink into myself, wishing the floor would swallow me whole.
